title: He Rauemi Tuku Iho, He Rauemi Āpōpō | Our Heritage, Our Future: Guardians of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, a significant waterway for Ngāti Kahungunu. They will examine historical accounts, local pūrākau (stories), and contemporary challenges facing the stream, connecting English language skills to real-world environmental and cultural stewardship in their own community.
learningObjectives: Analyze and compare historical and contemporary texts about the Mangakōtukutuku Stream to identify changes in perspective and language use over time.
Synthesize information from oral histories, written reports, and digital media to construct a coherent narrative about the stream's significance.
Compose a persuasive piece of writing (e.g., a formal letter, speech, or blog post) advocating for a specific action to protect the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, using evidence gathered from research.
Orally present findings and proposals to an audience, demonstrating effective use of language features and engaging delivery techniques.

activities.title: He Timatanga | The Beginning: Mapping Our Awa
activities.description: Students begin by physically visiting the Mangakōtukutuku Stream (or viewing high-quality drone footage and maps if a visit is not possible). They create an annotated map, labeling key features in both Te Reo Māori and English, and jotting down initial observations and questions. This grounds the learning in the actual place.

activities.title: He Kōrero Tawhito | Stories of the Past: Textual Analysis
activities.description: In small groups, students analyze a curated set of texts about the stream. This set includes a traditional pūrākau (provided by a local kaumātua), excerpts from early Pākehā settler diaries, and historical council reports. They use graphic organizers to compare the purpose, audience, and language features of each text.

activities.title: He Reo o Tēnei Wā | Voices of the Present: Listening and Synthesizing
activities.description: Students listen to a recorded interview with a representative from the local marae or a member of the Mangakōtukutuku Streamcare Group. They practice note-taking and summarization skills, identifying key concerns and hopes for the stream's future. They then synthesize this modern perspective with the historical texts from the previous activity.

activities.title: Tō Tātou Korero | Our Voice: Crafting Persuasion
activities.description: Using their research, each student chooses a specific issue facing the stream (e.g., pollution, invasive species, water quality) and a target audience (e.g., the local council, the college community, a local business). They then plan and draft a persuasive text (choosing an appropriate format like a formal letter, speech, or online article) advocating for a solution, ensuring they use evidence and appropriate language to persuade their chosen audience.

activities.title: Te Whakaatu i te Korero | Presenting Our Findings
activities.description: Students participate in a mini-symposium 'He Rongoā mō te Awa' (Healing for the Stream). They present the key arguments from their persuasive writing to a small group of peers and a invited community expert (e.g., from the Streamcare Group), focusing on clear and engaging oral delivery. Peers provide structured feedback.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu iwi website and historical archives for pūrākau and regional history.
Local expert: A kaumātua from a local marae or a member of the Mangakōtukutuku Streamcare Group for an interview or guest speaking.
Hawke's Bay Regional Council website, specifically its environmental monitoring data and reports on the Mangakōtukutuku catchment.
Digital recording equipment (tablets/phones) and editing software for conducting and processing interviews.

tasks: Annotated map and research notes from activities 1-3.
A final, polished persuasive text (e.g., letter, speech, blog post).
A reflection on the process of creating their text and how their understanding of kaitiakitanga has developed.

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features; Structure.
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features; Structure.
Social Sciences: Understand how people manage resources and care for the environment.
Key Competencies: Thinking; Using language, symbols, and texts; Participating and contributing.
